metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Drama 10 introduces students to the craft of dramatic performance and creation. Students build a repertoire of acting techniques, explore drama conventions and theatrical forms, collaborate to develop and present original and scripted works, and engage critically with theatre as an art form. First Peoples performance traditions and diverse theatrical cultures are central to the curriculum. Drama 10 opens pathways to Theatre Company, Theatre Production, Directing, Film and Television, and Musical Theatre.
metadata.general_description: A foundational senior drama course developing acting technique, character creation, improvisation, script interpretation, and collaborative theatrical production — opening pathways to advanced drama, film, and musical theatre.

big_ideas.statement: Drama communicates ideas, emotions, and perspectives through movement, sound, imagery, and language.
big_ideas.elaboration: Theatre is a multisensory art form — every element on stage, from the actor's body to the lighting design, contributes to meaning-making and audience experience.

big_ideas.statement: Active participation in drama creates personal and cultural connections and reveals insights into human experience.
big_ideas.elaboration: By inhabiting other perspectives and collaborating to tell stories, drama participants develop empathy, self-awareness, and a deeper understanding of the human condition across cultures and time periods.

big_ideas.statement: Drama offers dynamic ways of exploring our identity and sense of belonging.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through character creation, role play, and devised theatre, students explore who they are, where they belong, and how they are seen by the world — giving voice to personal and community narratives.

big_ideas.statement: Growth as an artist requires active engagement, risk taking, and reflection.
big_ideas.elaboration: Theatrical development demands courage — the willingness to try new approaches, accept vulnerability in performance, learn from failures, and engage seriously with feedback and self-evaluation.

big_ideas.statement: Drama cultivates collaboration through critical reflection, creative co-operation, and the exchange of ideas.
big_ideas.elaboration: Theatre is a fundamentally collaborative art — no production succeeds without mutual trust, shared vision, and the willingness to listen, adapt, and contribute to a collective creative process.

competencies.raw: Develop dramatic works expressing ideas and emotions through presentation or performance; build a repertoire of dramatic skills and techniques; intentionally select and combine drama conventions; experiment with props, processes, and technologies; create works with specific audiences in mind; improvise and embrace creative risks.
i_can_statements: I can create dramatic works that express specific ideas, emotions, and perspectives through performance.
I can build and apply a repertoire of acting techniques — voice, physicality, character, and improvisation.
I can intentionally select and combine drama conventions (e.g., freeze frame, hot seating, tableaux) for specific effects.
I can take creative risks in improvisation and original dramatic creation.
I can create dramatic works with a specific audience and purpose in mind.

competencies.raw: Analyze and respond to dramatic works using drama-specific language; apply feedback to refine creative ideas; connect dramatic works to personal experiences; examine social, cultural, historical, and environmental influences on drama.
i_can_statements: I can analyze and respond to dramatic works using accurate theatrical vocabulary.
I can apply feedback from peers and instructors to refine and improve my dramatic work.
I can connect the themes and perspectives of dramatic works to my own experiences and values.
I can examine the social, cultural, and historical contexts that shape different theatrical traditions.

competencies.raw: Document and respond to dramatic works through various media; use symbolism and imagery to convey meaning; express personal voice regarding environmental and social issues.
i_can_statements: I can document my dramatic process and product through journals, video, and written reflection.
I can use symbolism, imagery, and theatrical elements to convey complex meaning in my work.
I can use dramatic performance to give voice to environmental, social, and personal issues.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate responsibility in creating and performing; respect self, others, and audiences; explore First Peoples perspectives and cultural knowledge through drama; build family and community connections.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate responsibility and professionalism in the rehearsal and performance process.
I can show respect for myself, my ensemble, my community, and my audience.
I can explore First Peoples storytelling and performance traditions with respect and cultural sensitivity.
I can use drama to build connections with my family, school, and broader community.

areas.name: Drama Elements and Techniques
areas.raw: Drama elements; character development; movement, sound, image, and form.
topics: Elements of drama: character, tension, time, place, plot, mood, focus, and contrast
Voice for performance: resonance, projection, articulation, pitch, pace, and pause
Body for performance: gesture, posture, facial expression, stage movement, and spacing
Character creation: physical, vocal, and psychological dimensions; status and relationship
Movement and physicality: Laban Movement Analysis basics, physical characterization

areas.name: Improvisation and Devised Theatre
areas.raw: Strategies and techniques for rehearsal and performance; improvisation.
topics: Improvisation principles: 'yes, and...', listening, building scenes, and spontaneous response
Short-form and long-form improv games and structures
Devised theatre: creating original works from images, issues, texts, or objects
Drama conventions: tableaux, thought tracking, role on the wall, forum theatre, conscience alley

areas.name: Script and Performance
areas.raw: Drama forms and conventions; rehearsal and performance skills.
topics: Script analysis: given circumstances, objectives, obstacles, and actions
Script reading and interpretation: cold reading, script breakdown, and dramaturgical analysis
Scene study and performance: rehearsal process, blocking, and memorization strategies
Performance reflection: self-assessment, peer feedback, and post-show analysis

areas.name: Theatre History and Diverse Traditions
areas.raw: Cultural appropriation ethics; diverse theatrical traditions and First Peoples performance.
topics: Western theatre history: Greek, Elizabethan, Realism, Brechtian, and contemporary forms
First Peoples performance traditions: oral history, potlatch, ceremonial drama, and storytelling theatre
Diverse global theatrical traditions: commedia dell'arte, Noh, Kathakali, and African theatre
Contemporary theatre forms: applied theatre, community theatre, verbatim theatre, and documentary theatre
Cultural appropriation and representation in casting and storytelling
